_comment: SAMPLE ONLY — the non-claim doctrine is central to how OpenSoyce works. These are representative examples.
about: Non-claims are explicit statements of what OpenSoyce does NOT assert. They are not disclaimers — they are load-bearing doctrine. Every packet, attestation, and receipt carries non-claims so that buyers, security teams, and auditors understand exactly what the trust record does and does not prove.

non_claims.claim: OpenSoyce does not certify that software is safe, secure, or production-ready.
non_claims.explanation: A signed packet records a trust-decision chain. It does not replace a security audit, penetration test, or compliance review.

non_claims.claim: A packet does not prove the absence of vulnerabilities.
non_claims.explanation: 'No known intelligence recorded' means the record holds none — not that none exist. Threat intelligence is incomplete by definition.

non_claims.claim: A recorded remediation direction is not proof that remediation was completed.
non_claims.explanation: OpenSoyce records directions and evidence. It validates evidence is present and referenced — it does not verify the fix itself.

non_claims.claim: A signed packet does not prove what is currently deployed or running in production.
non_claims.explanation: Packets record declared artifact identity at ingestion time. Runtime observation (CI deployment observation) is a separate, reconcilable artifact — and it too does not prove live runtime state.

non_claims.claim: Signature validity proves packet integrity only — not software quality or absence of vulnerabilities.
non_claims.explanation: An Ed25519 signature over the bundle bytes proves the bundle has not been altered since signing. It says nothing about the content quality.

non_claims.claim: OpenSoyce does not verify the identity of the attester beyond what is self-asserted.
non_claims.explanation: Attester name and organization are self-declared. The attester signature proves they held the private key — not their identity, role authority, or competence.

non_claims.claim: A buyer verification receipt is buyer-generated and OpenSoyce-unsigned.
non_claims.explanation: The receipt records what was reviewed. Its trust derives from the signed bundle it points back to. A failed-verification receipt is a valid review record.

non_claims.claim: OpenSoyce does not replace an auditor or guarantee acceptance by any customer security review.
non_claims.explanation: Trust records are evidence inputs to review processes — not certification outputs.

why_non_claims_matter: The non-claim doctrine is what makes OpenSoyce honest. A trust record that overpromises is worse than no trust record. Non-claims are printed on every artifact so that reviewers know exactly what they are holding.

public_credibility_layer.note: Live verified proof artifacts and public key information are available at these URLs.
